summary: DHH argues that running a business purely by quantifiable metrics is impoverished, and that the best products come from leaders who care deeply about what they make. He critiques MBA-style management that reduces everything to abstract categories and spreadsheets. Founder-led companies maintain an edge because taste and judgment drive countless decisions that can't be captured in numbers. He advocates for 'vibe management' — trusting instincts and pursuing beauty alongside basic financial discipline.
key_points: Evaluating everything exclusively by quantifiable metrics is an impoverished way to run a business
Founder-led companies maintain an edge because genuine passion for the product shows up in countless value judgments that spreadsheets can't capture
Strong financial results are often derivatives of doing unquantifiable things well
Business owners should embrace 'vibe management' — following instincts and having fun — without apology
Financial basics (profitability, not overextending) provide the cushion needed to lead primarily on vision and vibes
money_quotes: Weighing everything exclusively by its quantifiable properties is an impoverished way to view business (and the world!).
The death of all that makes for a breakout product or service happens when the generic lingo of management theory takes over.

When there's someone at the top who actually gives a damn about cars, watches, bags, software, or whatever the hell the company makes, it shows up in a million value judgments that can't be quantified neatly on a spreadsheet.
Much of the time, those figures are derivatives of doing all the stuff that you can't compute and that won't quantify.

No business owner should ever apologize for having fun, following their instincts, or trusting that the numbers will eventually show that doing the right thing, the beautiful thing, the poetic thing is going to pay off somehow.
Once there's a bit of margin, you owe it to yourself to lean on that cushion and lead the business primarily on the basis of good vibes and a long vision.
key_insight: The best businesses are built on taste and conviction that can't be quantified — financial metrics are the byproduct, not the driver, of caring deeply about what you make.
